text: The surgeon uses a monopolar crown in coagulating mode to open the ovarian cortex of the right ovary, which contains dermoid cysts. A small amount of energy is applied to the ovary for a short duration. Using forceps, the surgeon performs traction and counter traction to dissect and increase the plane between the healthy ovarian tissue and the cyst, aiming to remove the entire cyst without rupture.

The surgeon continues the dissection of the ovarian dermoid cyst using traction and countertraction, applying minimal energy to preserve ovarian follicles. The cyst is progressively removed from its bed, with mechanical hemostasis performed using operative forceps. The final attachment of the ovary to the cyst is separated, allowing for safe removal of the cyst without compromising the residual ovary.

The surgeon performs hemostasis on the residual ovary using a small stitch with a two monocryl filament, avoiding bipolar coagulation. The stitch is applied without tying to minimize the risk of ovarian ischemia, aiming to restore normal anatomy. The knot is placed using an intra-corporeal knotting technique.

The surgeon cuts the excess stitch and places a classical handbag and a twelve millimeter draw card for protective ovarian cyst extraction. The dermoid cyst is placed inside the handbag and extracted from the left iliac trochanter. The cyst is emptied transabdominally inside the handbag, showing the final aspect of the surgery.
